core_claim: MiniPlasma is an LPE exploit that re-exploits a race condition in the Windows Cloud Files filter driver (cldflt.sys), effectively a return of CVE-2020-17103 that was believed patched in 2020. Released by Nightmare Eclipse as the sixth and final exploit in their wave. Silently addressed in the June 9, 2026 Patch Tuesday with no new CVE assigned.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released MiniPlasma as the sixth and final exploit in their wave. A local privilege escalation exploit targeting fully patched Windows 10 and 11 systems, silently addressed in the June 9, 2026 Patch Tuesday.

The flaw is not new. It maps to CVE-2020-17103, a race condition in the Windows Cloud Files filter driver (cldflt.sys) that was supposedly patched in December 2020. MiniPlasma proves the patch regressed or was never fully enforced. The original Google Project Zero proof-of-concept reportedly worked without modification on modern builds.

Researcher demonstrates exploitation on a machine with every June 2026 update installed. No new CVE was assigned. Vulnerability scanners that track current patch status have no signature for this. The 2020 record says patched. The system says otherwise.

Patch Tuesday says 'protected.' Scanners say 'clean.' But a six-year-old race condition in cldflt.sys is still reachable from a standard user account. No new CVE means no new scanner alert and no new vendor advisory. Defenders who rely on patch status alone have a real blind spot here.

The behavioral signal to watch: registry symbolic links being created in CloudFiles policy paths, and wermgr.exe executing outside standard system directories. Microsoft silently included a fix in June 9 Patch Tuesday. If you have not applied those updates, the exposure is active.

A fix without a new CVE is a scanner blind spot. Patched is not safe when an old bug quietly returns. Track silent fixes and lean on behavioral detection.

Patched does not mean immune. It means the attack surface has shifted.

Do you track regression vulnerabilities separately from new CVEs in your patch program?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[MiniPlasma, Nightmare Eclipse, Windows zero-day, LPE, fully patched, Patch Tuesday, CVE-2020-17103, cldflt.sys, Cloud Files, regression vulnerability, behavioral detection]
comment_prompt: Do you track regression vulnerabilities separately from new CVEs in your patch program?
alt_text: Title card: MiniPlasma escapes the patch, fully patched yet still root. It is an LPE on current Patch Tuesday with no new CVE, Nightmare Eclipse exploit six and the wave closer. A green plasma tendril slips through an Update shield checkmark to a SYSTEM prompt.

Nightmare Eclipse dropped MiniPlasma as the sixth and final exploit, targeting fully patched Windows 10 and 11 with June 2026 Patch Tuesday installed. No new CVE assigned. It maps to CVE-2020-17103, a regression in the Cloud Files filter driver believed patched in 2020.

MiniPlasma works on fully patched systems. A six-year-old race condition in cldflt.sys survived every update and still reaches SYSTEM. The 2020 patch either regressed or was never fully enforced. The researcher found the original Google Project Zero exploit worked without changes.

The mechanism: a race condition in the Cloud Files filter driver, cldflt.sys, exploits the HsmOsBlockPlaceholderAccess routine to escalate from a standard user account to SYSTEM. No secondary step required. A race condition arrow points from a standard user icon to a SYSTEM crown.

Where defenders miss it: Patch Tuesday says protected and scanners say clean, but no new CVE was assigned so there is no new scanner signature. Scanners that only check current patch status miss regression vulnerabilities entirely. A scanner report shows clean under a red regression arrow.

Mitigation: monitor CloudFiles registry symbolic links in the BlockedApps policy key, watch for wermgr.exe running outside standard system directories, and apply the silent June 9, 2026 Patch Tuesday fix. A behavioral detection rule flags the CloudFiles registry key alongside wermgr.

Patched does not mean immune. It means the attack surface has shifted. A Windows Update shield, half with a green checkmark and half with a green plasma tendril slipping through to form a SYSTEM crown.
Closing question: Do you track regression vulnerabilities separately from new CVEs in your patch program? A behavioral detection rule for CloudFiles registry changes sits on a clean dark end-card.

sources.supports: Sixth and final exploit in wave; targets fully patched Windows 10/11; single race condition in cldflt.sys (HsmOsBlockPlaceholderAccess routine); maps to CVE-2020-17103; original Google Project Zero PoC worked without changes; researcher unsure if patch never applied or silently rolled back; no new CVE assigned.

Sixth of six publicly released Windows exploits by Nightmare Eclipse without prior Microsoft coordination; CVE-2020-17103 regression in Cloud Files filter driver; fully updated Windows 11, Server 2022, and Server 2025 vulnerable; Microsoft scheduled patch for June 9, 2026; behavioral detection indicators including CloudFiles BlockedApps registry symbolic link and wermgr.exe outside system paths.

sources.source: CSO Online: Patched Windows bug resurfaces 6 years later as working SYSTEM-level exploit
sources.supports: Race condition in cldflt.sys HsmOsBlockPlaceholderAccess routine; originally reported by Google Project Zero James Forshaw; patched December 2020; confirmed still exploitable on fully patched systems with May 2026 updates; Will Dormann confirmed buggy behavior; MiniPlasma is sixth in a wave beginning with BlueHammer.

Windows Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability; originally published December 2020; CISA-ADP added MiniPlasma GitHub PoC reference in May 2026 confirming the regression; CVSS 7.8 HIGH.

sources.source: Tenable: Windows Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver EoP (MiniPlasma) (CVE-2020-17103)
sources.supports: Microsoft originally addressed CVE-2020-17103 in December 2020; fix has since regressed; publicly available PoC demonstrates vulnerability again exploitable on currently patched hosts as of May 2026; Nessus plugin 316497 published May 22, 2026.

narration.text: MiniPlasma is a local privilege escalation exploit that works on fully patched Windows 10 and 11, even with every June 2026 update applied.
Nightmare Eclipse released it as the sixth and final exploit in their wave, and Microsoft silently addressed it in the June 9, 2026 Patch Tuesday with no new CVE.
The flaw maps to CVE-2020-17103, a race condition in the Windows Cloud Files filter driver, cldflt.sys, that was supposed to have been fixed back in December 2020.
The researcher found that the original Google Project Zero proof-of-concept worked without any changes on modern Windows builds, meaning the patch either regressed or was never fully enforced.
Because no new CVE was assigned, vulnerability scanners that check patch status have no new signature to fire, leaving defenders with a genuine blind spot on otherwise current systems.
The behavioral signals to watch are registry symbolic links created in CloudFiles policy paths, and wermgr.exe executing from outside its standard system directories.
A fix without a new CVE is a scanner blind spot, and this is exactly the kind of silent regression that patch-status dashboards miss entirely.
If your team tracks regression vulnerabilities separately from new CVEs, share your approach in the comments.

beats.caption: MiniPlasma escapes patch. Fully patched. Root.
Wave closer. June patch installed. No new CVE. Maps to CVE-2020-17103.
Six-year-old race condition. cldflt.sys. Survived every patch.
Original 2020 PoC still works. Patch regressed. SYSTEM.
No new CVE. Scanner clean. 2020 record says patched.
CloudFiles registry symlink. wermgr.exe out of path. Silent June fix.
Patched not immune. Surface shifted. Pattern persists.
